title_en: The Four Caliphs and the First Islamic Century
title_id: Empat Khalifah dan Abad Pertama Islam
content_en:
Ibn al-Athir's annalistic coverage of the caliphate period begins at 11 AH with the death of the Prophet and the succession of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. His treatment of the Rightly-Guided Caliphs (al-Khulafa' al-Rashidun) follows the mainstream Sunni historiographical tradition: all four caliphs are treated with respect as the rightful successors to the Prophet in order, their decisions are understood as those of the best generation of Muslims, and the first civil wars (fitan) are presented as genuine disputes among people of good faith rather than as evidence of fundamental corruption in the early community. This Sunni framework distinguishes Ibn al-Athir's account from the Shi'i historical tradition, which would frame the same events through the lens of 'Ali's priority, and from the critical perspectives of some later historians who saw the early disputes as the source of enduring Muslim weaknesses.

The caliphate of Abu Bakr (11-13 AH) is covered with attention to the Ridda wars, which Ibn al-Athir treats as the decisive test of the young Islamic state's survival. The decision to fight the apostate tribes and those who refused zakah, championed by Abu Bakr against some initial hesitation from other companions, is presented as a moment of prophetic foresight that secured the unity of the Arabian Peninsula and enabled the subsequent conquests. 'Umar's caliphate (13-23 AH) receives the most extensive treatment of the four, reflecting the extraordinary scale of the Islamic expansion during this decade: the conquests of Iraq, Syria, Persia, and Egypt that transformed the Islamic state from an Arabian polity into a world empire. Ibn al-Athir covers the major battles, the administrative organization of the conquered territories, and 'Umar's institutional innovations such as the diwan and the garrison cities.

The caliphates of 'Uthman (23-35 AH) and 'Ali (35-40 AH) present the most challenging narrative for any Sunni historian, and Ibn al-Athir handles them with characteristic care. The assassination of 'Uthman, the first civil war, the Battle of the Camel (in which 'Ali's forces defeated those led by 'A'ishah, Talhah, and al-Zubayr), the Battle of Siffin, and the arbitration crisis that produced the Khawarij are all narrated through a careful assembly of transmitted accounts. Ibn al-Athir maintains his respect for all the major companions while acknowledging that a genuine political crisis divided the community. He avoids the partisan framing that characterized both pro-'Alid and anti-'Alid historiography, presenting the events as a tragic but understandable result of the pressures facing a rapidly expanding community.

The early Umayyad period, beginning with Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan's consolidation of power in 40 AH and the 'Am al-Jama'ah (Year of Reconciliation) in which Hasan ibn 'Ali ceded his claim to the caliphate, continues the annalistic method through the subsequent generations. The killing of Husayn ibn 'Ali at Karbala' in 61 AH is treated as a genuine tragedy, and Ibn al-Athir's account of the events leading to it and the aftermath conveys the weight of the event in Islamic historical memory. The Umayyad expansion into North Africa, Spain, Central Asia, and Sind is covered systematically through the later decades of the first Islamic century, presenting the conquests as a continuation of the providential expansion that began under 'Umar. By the end of the first century AH, the Islamic world stretched from the Atlantic to the borders of China, a transformation that Ibn al-Athir covers with appropriate attention to both its military and its civilizational dimensions.
content_id:
Liputan annalistik Ibn al-Athir tentang periode kekhalifahan dimulai pada tahun 11 AH dengan wafatnya Nabi ﷺ dan suksesi Abu Bakar al-Siddiq (ra). Perlakuannya terhadap Khulafa ar-Rashidun mengikuti tradisi historiografi Sunni arus utama: semua empat khalifah diperlakukan dengan hormat sebagai penerus sah Nabi ﷺ secara berurutan, keputusan-keputusan mereka dipahami sebagai keputusan generasi Muslim terbaik, dan perang-perang saudara pertama (fitnah) disajikan sebagai perselisihan nyata di antara orang-orang yang beritikad baik, bukan sebagai bukti korupsi fundamental dalam komunitas awal. Kerangka Sunni ini membedakan narasi Ibn al-Athir dari tradisi historiografi Syiah, yang akan membingkai peristiwa-peristiwa yang sama melalui lensa prioritas Ali (ra), dan dari perspektif kritis sebagian sejarawan belakangan yang melihat perselisihan awal sebagai sumber kelemahan Muslim yang berkelanjutan.

Kekhalifahan Abu Bakar al-Siddiq (ra) (11–13 AH) dicakup dengan perhatian pada perang riddah, yang Ibn al-Athir perlakukan sebagai ujian menentukan bagi kelangsungan negara Islam yang masih muda. Keputusan untuk memerangi suku-suku murtad dan mereka yang menolak membayar zakat, yang diperjuangkan oleh Abu Bakar al-Siddiq (ra) di tengah keraguan awal sebagian sahabat lainnya, disajikan sebagai momen pandangan jauh ke depan yang mengamankan persatuan Jazirah Arab dan memungkinkan penaklukan-penaklukan berikutnya. Kekhalifahan Umar ibn al-Khattab (ra) (13–23 AH) mendapatkan perlakuan paling ekstensif di antara keempatnya, mencerminkan skala ekspansi Islam yang luar biasa selama dekade ini: penaklukan Irak, Syam, Persia, dan Mesir yang mengubah negara Islam dari sebuah politi Arabia menjadi kekaisaran dunia. Ibn al-Athir mencakup pertempuran-pertempuran besar, organisasi administratif wilayah-wilayah yang ditaklukkan, dan inovasi-inovasi institusional Umar (ra) seperti diwan dan kota-kota garnisun.

Kekhalifahan Utsman (ra) (23–35 AH) dan Ali (ra) (35–40 AH) menghadirkan narasi yang paling menantang bagi sejarawan Sunni manapun, dan Ibn al-Athir menanganinya dengan kehati-hatian yang khas. Pembunuhan Utsman (ra), perang saudara pertama, Perang Jamal (di mana pasukan Ali (ra) mengalahkan yang dipimpin oleh Aisyah (ra), Thalhah, dan al-Zubayr), Perang Shiffin, dan krisis arbitrase yang melahirkan kaum Khawarij semuanya dinarasikan melalui kumpulan cermat narasi-narasi yang ditransmisikan. Ibn al-Athir mempertahankan rasa hormatnya kepada semua sahabat besar sambil mengakui bahwa krisis politik nyata membelah komunitas. Beliau menghindari pembingkaian partisan yang menjadi ciri khas historiografi pro-Alid maupun anti-Alid, menyajikan peristiwa-peristiwa sebagai hasil yang tragis namun dapat dipahami dari tekanan-tekanan yang dihadapi komunitas yang berkembang pesat.

Periode Umayyah awal, dimulai dengan konsolidasi kekuasaan Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan pada tahun 40 AH dan 'Am al-Jama'ah (Tahun Persatuan) di mana Hasan ibn Ali (ra) menyerahkan klaimnya atas kekhalifahan, melanjutkan metode annalistik melalui generasi-generasi berikutnya. Pembunuhan Husain ibn Ali (ra) di Karbala' pada tahun 61 AH diperlakukan sebagai tragedi nyata, dan narasi Ibn al-Athir tentang peristiwa-peristiwa yang mengarah padanya serta akibat-akibatnya menyampaikan bobot peristiwa itu dalam memori historis Islam. Ekspansi Umayyah ke Afrika Utara, Spanyol, Asia Tengah, dan Sind dicakup secara sistematis melalui dekade-dekade belakangan abad pertama Islam, menyajikan penaklukan-penaklukan sebagai kelanjutan dari ekspansi yang telah ditakdirkan yang dimulai pada masa Umar (ra). Pada akhir abad pertama AH, dunia Islam terbentang dari Samudra Atlantik hingga perbatasan Tiongkok, sebuah transformasi yang Ibn al-Athir cakup dengan perhatian yang tepat pada dimensi militer maupun peradabannya.
content_ar: Ibn al-Athir's annalistic coverage of the الخلافة period begins at 11 AH with the الموت of النبي ﷺ and the succession of أبو بكر al-Siddiq. His treatment of the Rightly-Guided Caliphs (al-Khulafa' al-Rashidun) follows the mainstream Sunni historiographical التراث: all four caliphs are treated with الاحترام as the rightful التابعين to النبي ﷺ in order, their decisions are understood as those of the best generation of المسلمون, and the أولاً civil wars (fitan) are presented as genuine disputes among people of الخير الإيمان rather than as evidence of الأساسي corruption in the early الجماعة. This Sunni framework distinguishes Ibn al-Athir's account from the Shi'i historical التراث, which would frame the same events through the lens of 'علي's priority, and from the البالغ الأهمية perspectives of some later historians who saw the early disputes as the source of enduring مسلم weaknesses. The الخلافة of أبو بكر (11-13 AH) is covered with attention to the Ridda wars, which Ibn al-Athir treats as the decisive الاختبار of the young Islamic state's survival. The decision to fight the apostate tribes and those who refused الزكاة, championed by أبو بكر against some initial hesitation from other الصحابة, is presented as a moment of prophetic foresight that secured the unity of the Arabian Peninsula and enabled the subsequent conquests. 'عمر's الخلافة (13-23 AH) receives the most extensive treatment of the four, reflecting the extraordinary scale of the Islamic expansion خلال this decade: the conquests of Iraq, Syria, Persia, and Egypt that transformed the Islamic state from an Arabian polity into a الدنيا empire. Ibn al-Athir covers the major battles, the administrative organization of the conquered territories, and 'عمر's institutional innovations مثل the diwan and the garrison cities. The caliphates of 'عثمان (23-35 AH) and 'علي (35-40 AH) present the most challenging narrative for any Sunni historian, and Ibn al-Athir handles them with characteristic care. The assassination of 'عثمان, the أولاً civil war, the Battle of the Camel (in which 'علي's forces defeated those led by 'A'ishah, Talhah, and al-Zubayr), the Battle of Siffin, and the arbitration crisis that produced the Khawarij are all narrated through a careful assembly of transmitted accounts. Ibn al-Athir maintains his الاحترام for all the major الصحابة بينما acknowledging that a genuine political crisis divided the الجماعة. He avoids the partisan framing that characterized both pro-'Alid and anti-'Alid historiography, presenting the events as a tragic but understandable result of the pressures facing a rapidly expanding الجماعة. The early Umayyad period, beginning with Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan's consolidation of power in 40 AH and the 'Am al-Jama'ah (Year of Reconciliation) in which حسن ibn 'علي ceded his claim to the الخلافة, continues the annalistic method through the subsequent generations.
